critique.summary: The article is a detailed personal account of building a homebrew 8-bit CPU called WCPU-1, describing the challenges faced transitioning from simulation to hardware, including design flaws, timing issues, and debugging efforts. The author also discusses the custom toolchain, instruction set, and plans for future improvements and open sourcing.
mainPoints: The author simulated the CPU design before building the physical hardware but encountered many unexpected issues during the build.
Several custom PCBs were designed and fabricated, including an EEPROM programmer, a generic register board, and a control module board, each with specific challenges and fixes.
Hardware issues included timing glitches, floating pins, inverted signals, and soldering errors that required extensive debugging.
The CPU runs at 1 MHz, supports 23 instructions with 3 addressing modes, and is Turing complete with 256 bytes of RAM.
A custom Python-based microcode generator and assembler were developed to program the CPU, with an Arduino Mega used temporarily as a loader.
The project is ongoing with plans to improve the design, remove the Arduino loader, add output capabilities, and eventually open source all materials.
The author shares an honest review of PCBWay's fabrication service, noting high quality but some shipping errors that were quickly resolved.
critique.truthfulness: The article is a first-person detailed project log of a homebrew CPU build, and the technical details and challenges described align with common issues faced in hardware prototyping and digital logic design. The author provides specific chip models, design decisions, and debugging steps that are consistent with known electronics engineering practices. The description of glitches, timing issues, and the use of EEPROMs and registers matches documented behavior of such components. The review of PCBWay's service appears candid and plausible. Since the article is a personal project report rather than a news report, the claims are credible as experiential facts.
considerations: The article does not provide external validation or third-party verification of the CPU's functionality beyond the author's own testing and videos.
Performance metrics such as speed and stability are based on the author's observations and may vary with different builds or conditions.
The project is a prototype and not a commercial or standardized CPU, so some design choices prioritize learning and experimentation over efficiency or robustness.
Future improvements and open sourcing are planned but not yet realized, so the current state is incomplete.
The article assumes some technical background in digital electronics and may not be accessible to all readers.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around a homebrew CPU project, with commenters sharing technical advice, personal experiences, and encouragement. Some provide detailed hardware suggestions, while others reflect on the challenges of physical CPU builds versus simulations.

comments.author: P-Nuts
comments.text: To get both blinkenlights for registers and tri-state for bus driving, use two ’574 chips in parallel rather than a ’377 behind a ’245. Tie the clock and input lines together on both. Tie the output enable low on the one driving the blinkenlights. This way the chip that the rest of the CPU depends on doesn’t have the extra work of driving any load and you only have one chip’s worth of propagation delays.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a specific hardware recommendation that is technically sound and practical for reducing propagation delays and load on the CPU.

comments.author: artemonster
comments.text: I always applaud homebrew cpu designs but after doing so many myself I would reaaaaly advice to stay away from dip chips/breadboards/wirewraps and any attempts to put it into real physical world. Taking a build out of a logisim/verilog to real world in chips sucks away all the fun about cpu design - suddenly you have to deal with invisible issues like timing, glitchy half-dead chip, bad wire connection, etc. these are not challenges, just mundane dull work. The only exception to „stay in the sim“ rule is if you want to make an „art statement“, i.e. like BMOW (or my relay cpu https://github.com/artemonster/relay-cpu/blob/main/images/fr... /shamelessplug)
comments.analysis: The author shares experienced-based advice about the difficulties of physical CPU builds, emphasizing the mundane challenges that detract from design fun, which aligns with common hardware development realities.

comments.author: jacquesm
comments.text:
Hi Will, absolutely amazing this, I love the 'money shot' up front, there are days my desk and yours could be swapped without either of us realizing right away what happened.

The Rigol deserves a blog post of its own, I've got one too and the better I get in using it the more I'm amazed at what it can do.

I've run into the same 'all you can get is SMD' which is fine for when you're finished but a lot harder while you're still figuring things out. This is where 'proper engineers' can go straight to the finish line and I always struggle.

You also develop some kind of sixth sense for when something is misbehaving. If you haven't read it yet, 'The Soul of a New Machine' might be to your liking.

best of luck with your project!

Oh, and I did read all the way to the end.
comments.analysis: This comment offers encouragement and personal reflections on similar experiences, adding a human element and recommending relevant literature, which enriches the discussion.

comments.author: tchanukvadze
comments.text:
Hello Will, interesting read, and happy to see 8-bit breadboard builds on HN. I used to follow Ben and James years ago and built a fully functional 256-byte ROM/RAM cpu that could accept instructions from Arduino as a IO input. I gave up on extending output to add a mini OLED.
Cool to see your project and can't wait for part 4.
comments.analysis: The commenter shares relevant personal experience with similar projects, contributing positively to the community knowledge and encouraging continuation.

comments.author: Mercuriusdream
comments.text:
Also tried to homebrew CPUs before but couldn't even start due to a wall of things to prepare;

Seeing this is just amazing to be honest. Wish you a luck on your project!
comments.analysis: This comment expresses admiration and empathy, reflecting common beginner frustrations and offering moral support.

comments.author: Taniwha
comments.text: Of course while you're doing the next version you should knock out a tiny tapeout version, it should easily fit in a single cell (maybe 2 if you want to push the 256 byte sram in as well)
comments.analysis: The suggestion to create a tapeout version is constructive and plausible given the project scope, though it is brief and less detailed.

comments.author: komali2
comments.text:
> It’s a standalone tool that lives outside the computer. I put the EEPROM into the socket, and connect via serial to my laptop to upload the binary files.

Huh, I guess I never really thought about it, but how did they program the first CPUs? Like how did they overcome the chicken/egg situation?
comments.analysis: This comment poses a genuine question about historical CPU programming methods, contributing curiosity but no direct technical insight.
